title: The Macro Minute: How should investors respond to the US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Monday,
April 13th, 2026. Hope everyone had a
great weekend. So, as always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's lead-off morning note. So, let's
dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, how should investors
respond to the US blockade of the Strait
of Hormuz? Our answer is positive
developments positive developments
signal that the US-Israel-Iran conflict
is still on track for a positive
resolution. The blockade merely confirms
our expectation for a series of viable
dips due to brinksmanship throughout the
negotiations.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Uh nothing
actionable with respect to our Bayesian
priors in the growth cycle. Regarding
the inflation cycle, President Trump
ordered a blockade of the Strait of
Hormuz following an impasse in US-Iran
negotiations, signaling a re-escalation
of geopolitical risk. If enforced, the
blockade would disrupt Iranian cargo,
the only remaining uninterrupted crude
oil shipments throughout the conflict.
Uh Iran declared it would target all
ports in and near the Persian Gulf if
its own shipping infrastructure is
threatened, according to the state-run
IRIB news. Iran's armed forces stated
Monday that regional port security is,
quote, either for everyone or no one,
signaling a maximalist reaction
function. Iran characterized the US
threat to block the Strait as a, quote,
act of piracy while reaffirming
intentions to maintain permanent control
over the strategic waterway
post-conflict.
Nothing actionable uh with respect to
the monetary policy and fiscal policy
cycles. Regarding the liquidity cycle,
in China, the March credit data did not
support our outlook for firming growth.
The Chinese economy remains mired in a
structural liquidity trap for two
reasons. Number one, widespread mis-
widespread capital misallocation due to
decades of overinvestment. And number
two, widespread adverse selection due to
excessive private sector debt
accumulation. China's March credit data
incrementally confirm the PBOC's prudent
reaction function, which is why the
Chinese economy is already on the wrong
side of its most muted credit impulse on
record. The good news is that the policy
support of the PBOC is providing is
creating excess liquidity in the Chinese
financial sector, which is positive for
global liquidity. Indeed, global
liquidity is still in a multi-year
uptrend, according to the 42 macro
global liquidity proxy, which is the
aggregated sum of the major central bank
balance sheets, global broad money
supply, and global fiat uh FX reserves.
Uh in terms of the positioning cycle, uh
the US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz
risks intensifying US-China tensions,
given China's reliance on Iranian oil,
while simultaneously exacerbating an
already fragile global energy supply
backdrop. The blockade also risks
intensifying the developing energy price
crisis in the US. Uh quote, uh enjoy the
current pump figures with the so-called
blockade. Soon you'll be nostalgic for 4
to 5% gas, Iranian Parliament Speaker
Mohammad Qalibaf posted on X,
underscoring upside risk to US gasoline
prices. Qalibaf led Iran's delegation in
Islamabad in talks that collapsed over
irreconcilable differences tied to the
trajectory of Iran's nuclear program.
Despite no agreement on when to hold
subsequent negotiations, Foreign
Ministry Spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei uh
confirmed that the unresolved disputes
only focus on two or three key points.
So, this is what we mean when we're
talking about positive developments. You
went from seven points
>> [laughter]
>> and fifth 10-point uh plan and a
15-point plan on the US side and 10 on
the Iranian side to now they're only
talking about two or three points.
That's a material move forward in
negotiations. Um
getting back into this, uh all told,
these developments signal that the
US-Israel-Iran conflict is still on
track for a positive resolution. The
blockade merely confirms our expectation
for a series of viable dips due to
brinksmanship throughout the
negotiations. Of course, there's going
to be multiple rounds of negotiations,
and of course, there's going to be
brinksmanship. This is a negotiation
between the US and Iran. But what we
were talking about last week, uh in our
opinion, the uh the the Iran's uh
willingness to open the Strait of Hormuz
throughout the negotiations uh as part
as as as part of the ceasefire, in my
opinion, that signals that their
ultimate their end goal their end game
is not keeping the Strait of Hormuz
closed. You wouldn't start a negotiation
with giving up your number one thing
that you want to have negotiated uh in
the negotiations by giving up the number
one thing you want in the negotiations.
I mean, that just like that nobody
negotiates that way. So, in our opinion,
uh we think we've moved past the uh peak
point of uncertainty regarding the um
the the the the the the the restricted
uh petroleum product supply and the
restricted uh supply of fertilizer uh in
the uh that is currently trapped in the
Strait of Hormuz. Uh in our view,
there's going to be a lumpy trend higher
in the uh the shipment of those supplies
uh in the coming weeks and months. Uh
it's not going to be easy. There will be
more brinksmanship, but in our opinion,
uh we think that we're we're past the
worst of this conflict. If we're wrong
on that, we'll be wrong. You know, you
got to take your risk. You got to plant
your flag if you want to make money in
financial markets. You can't just
equivocate like an economist. So, let's
keep moving here. Uh as always, we'll
wrap up with a question uh from our
community. Uh this one's titled material
change in situation. Uh it says, "Hello,
community. Regarding uh up on the
changes this AM re DJT instructing the
blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, uh
reading up Sorry, my apologies. Reading
up. Uh that is seemingly a material
change in the situation, eh? Wonder how
this can get reconciled with yesterday's
around the horn, which you may not
finish yet uh this morning, where DD
discussed brinksmanship {slash}
gamesmanship, etc. Uh DJT's instruction
of a blockade seems more than that, no?
Wouldn't this go back to what
effectively caused the risk-off phase
transition in the first place, which is
the closure of the Strait, barrels of
oil not moving freely, causing inflation
to spike, uh etc.? Curious of your
thoughts." So, yeah, I mean, if this if
So, my general take on this is if if the
if this devolves beyond what we
currently expect, then we're obviously
going to return uh back uh to a a
risk-off uh market regime condition. But
in our opinion,
just based on what I just said,
it's highly unlikely that the durable,
long-term closure of the Strait of
Hormuz, which is the key tail risk here,
is particularly from the perspective of
the global liquidity cycle and the
global dollar recycling flows from the
uh Persian Gulf economies and the
international investment surplus flows
in Gulf economies that aren't getting uh
the GDP that they need uh to generate
profits and household savings that they
further reinvest and recycle uh into uh
international capital markets. Same deal
with the Asian economies that are
extremely reliant uh on that crude oil.
Same deal with the European economies
that are extremely reliant on that crude
oil. Japan, China, Europe. Uh so,
Germany, um China, and Japan are the
three largest net international
investment uh surplus economies in the
world, and they're each uh very reliant
on on on on Middle Eastern uh petroleum
products uh to generate economic
activity. And so, you know, that is the
ultimate tail risk in terms of the 6 to
um you know, on the low end, 6 to 8% on
the high end, 8 to 11% uh hit to global
GDP if the Strait of Hormuz remains
closed. But again, as I said, if you're
negotiating with a a counterparty an
adversary, and you give up the number
one thing you want in the negotiation as
part of the negotiation as as as as a
reason to start negotiating, to me that
signals that that's not their ultimate
goal. So, if we're right on that game
theory view viewpoint, um then
ultimately I think we're going to
resolve this um
this conflict uh uh uh well. So, you
know, if I were you guys, I would just
try to put earmuffs on, use these um you
know, these these these episodes of
brinksmanship, these episodes of
gamesmanship as an opportunity to buy
the dip, in our opinion, um until
further notice, you know, until our our
our regime signals uh say something
different. So, we're wrapping it there.
Jerry Cell here, uh communicating our
